_meta.note: Source language. The complete base every other pack layers onto. All public copy here is evidence-gated and em-dash-free per CONCORD guardrails.

site.name: ronutz
site.tagline: Network and security tools that run on your machine, not someone else's cloud.

languageSwitcher.label: Language
languageSwitcher.stubNotice: This language is not translated yet. Showing English.

hero.title: The network and security toolbox that keeps your data on your machine.
hero.subtitle: Subnet math, DNS and email diagnostics, certificate inspection, and more, computed locally in your browser. No upload, no logging, no account required for the everyday tools.

credibility.title: Built by someone who has done the work since 1996.
credibility.body: Thirty years in enterprise networking and security, delivered as a working instructor rather than a marketing page. Authorized across six vendors, so the comparisons here are made honestly, not sold.

pillars.title: Four platforms, taught and held current.

privacy.title: Why local-first matters for security work.
privacy.body: When you paste a configuration, a packet capture, or a certificate into a tool, that data often contains secrets. Here, the everyday tools run entirely in your browser. The input never leaves your device, so there is nothing to log, leak, or subpoena.

toolPreview.title: Try a tool right now.
toolPreview.body: The CIDR calculator below runs locally. Type a network and prefix to see the address range, host count, and mask. Nothing is sent anywhere.

provenance.title: Credits & sources
provenance.disclaimer: This tool is built from public vendor documentation and may not be fully accurate or current. For production use, always verify against the vendor's official documentation.

funding.title: Support this tool
funding.pitch: This tool is free and built to stay that way. If it earns a place in your workflow, you can help fund its upkeep and what comes next.

as3-explainer-validator.name: AS3 declaration explainer
as3-explainer-validator.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/appsvcs/declare and it reads it back: whether it is a full AS3 request or an ADC-only declaration, the schemaVersion and metadata, and the Tenant to Application to resource tree with every class explained, while checking the structural rules F5 documents. Runs entirely in your browser.
as3-explainer-validator.inputLabel: An AS3 request or ADC declaration (JSON)
as3-explainer-validator.inputPlaceholder:
Paste an AS3 declaration, for example:
{ "class": "AS3", "action": "deploy", "declaration": { "class": "ADC", "schemaVersion": "3.0.0", "MyTenant": { "class": "Tenant", "MyApp": { "class": "Application", "service": { "class": "Service_HTTP", "virtualAddresses": ["10.0.0.1"] } } } } }
as3-explainer-validator.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
as3-explainer-validator.empty: Paste an AS3 request or an ADC declaration to see its structure explained and checked.
as3-explainer-validator.scopeNote: A structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator: it checks the documented structural rules and explains the classes, but a declaration that passes here can still be rejected by AS3 itself. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

kind.as3Request: This is a full AS3 request (class AS3)
kind.adcOnly: This is an ADC-only declaration (class ADC, no AS3 wrapper)
kind.notAs3: This is not an AS3 or ADC declaration

finding.parseError: That looks like JSON but could not be parsed
finding.notAs3: The top-level object has no class of AS3 or ADC, so this is not an AS3 declaration. An AS3 request starts with class AS3; an ADC declaration starts with class ADC.
finding.adcOnly: This is an ADC-only declaration (no AS3 wrapper), so the action and persist options are not available. Wrap it in a class AS3 object to use them.
finding.missingSchema: The ADC declaration has no schemaVersion. AS3 requires it, and it is decoupled from the AS3 release version.
finding.noTenant: The declaration has no Tenant. A declaration must contain at least one object with class Tenant, which becomes a BIG-IP partition.
finding.emptyTenant: Tenant {tenant} has no Application. Each tenant must contain at least one object with class Application.
finding.emptyApp: Application {app} in tenant {tenant} has no resources. An application must contain at least one ADC resource, for example a Service or Pool.
finding.templateMismatch: Application {app} in tenant {tenant} uses template {template}, which requires a matching {needs} object named service (formerly serviceMain), but none was found.
finding.invalidName: The name "{name}" in {where} is not a valid AS3 name: names are 1 to 64 characters, alphanumeric or underscore, and must start with a letter.

as3-explainer-validator.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog; still shown)

base64.name: Base64, Base32, Hex & Percent Codec
base64.blurb: Encode text to Base64, URL-safe Base64, Base32, hexadecimal, or percent-encoding, and decode any of them back. Tolerant of missing padding and whitespace, and it flags binary (non-UTF-8) results. Runs entirely in your browser.
base64.runsLocally: Encoding and decoding run locally. Nothing you paste leaves your browser.

decodeErrors.invalid-characters: That input contains characters outside the selected codec's alphabet.
decodeErrors.invalid-length: That input is not a valid length for the selected codec.
decodeErrors.invalid-escape: That input has a malformed percent-escape (a % not followed by two hex digits).

base64.decodedBytes: Decoded {bytes, plural, one {# byte} other {# bytes}}.
base64.notUtf8: Decoded {bytes, plural, one {# byte} other {# bytes}}, but the result is not valid UTF-8 text (likely binary).
base64.layersOptionTitle: {n, plural, =1 {Single pass (no extra layers)} other {Apply the codec # times: n-encode, or peel # layers when decoding}}

cert-renewal-planner.name: Certificate renewal planner
cert-renewal-planner.blurb: Work out a TLS certificate's validity, whether it fits the CA/Browser Forum 47-day schedule, and the renewal cadence it implies — all offline.
cert-renewal-planner.runsLocally: Everything runs in your browser; nothing is uploaded.
cert-renewal-planner.maxForPhase: Maximum for its issuance date: {max} days.
cert-renewal-planner.overByText: {days} days over the {max}-day maximum for its issuance date — a public CA would not issue it.
cert-renewal-planner.renewalsValue: About {n} renewals per year at this validity.
cert-renewal-planner.dcvText: Domain validation (DCV) data can be reused for {days} days.
cert-renewal-planner.siiText: Organization identity (OV/EV SII) can be reused for {days} days.
cert-renewal-planner.scheduleIntro: Public TLS maximums key off the certificate's issuance date:
cert-renewal-planner.projectionIntro: Renewals per certificate per year, as the caps tighten:
cert-renewal-planner.note_overCap: This validity exceeds the {max}-day maximum for its issuance date.
cert-renewal-planner.note_future47: Compliant today, but longer than the eventual 47-day cap — expect more frequent renewals as the schedule advances.
cert-renewal-planner.note_publicOnly: These caps apply to publicly trusted TLS certificates only. Private or internal PKI is not governed by SC-081v3.
cert-renewal-planner.note_automate: At these cadences, automate issuance and renewal with ACME (RFC 8555) and ARI (RFC 9773); manual renewal does not scale.
cert-renewal-planner.err_empty: Enter both the issue and expiry dates.
cert-renewal-planner.err_order: The expiry date must be after the issue date.

cidr.name: CIDR / Subnet Calculator
cidr.blurb: Break down any IPv4 CIDR block into network and broadcast addresses, usable host range, host count, and netmask. Runs entirely in your browser.
cidr.title: CIDR Calculator
cidr.description: Compute the network and broadcast addresses, usable host range, host count, and netmask for an IPv4 CIDR block. Runs locally in your browser.
cidr.runsLocally: Runs locally. Nothing is sent to any server.
cidr.bitsNote: The highlighted bits identify the network — the rest address hosts within it.

errors.empty: Enter a CIDR block to calculate.
errors.invalid: That does not look like a valid CIDR block. Try a format like 192.168.1.0/24.

modeDesc.subnet: Analyze a single IPv4 CIDR block: network, broadcast, mask, and host range.
modeDesc.vlsm: Carve variable-length subnets from a parent block to fit a list of host counts.
modeDesc.supernet: Summarize a list of prefixes into the smallest set of covering blocks.
modeDesc.overlap: Find overlaps and containment between prefixes, plus gaps within an optional scope.

supernet.label: Prefixes to aggregate
supernet.singleExtra: Adds {extra} extra addresses beyond the inputs.

overlap.label: Prefixes to check
overlap.noGaps: No gaps in the given scope.

err.ipv4: Not a valid IPv4 address. Use four octets 0-255, like 192.168.1.0.
err.octet: An octet is out of range (each must be 0-255).
err.prefix: The prefix length must be between 0 and 32.
err.tooMany: Too many entries. Trim the list and try again.
err.noReqs: Add at least one subnet requirement with a host count.
err.noPrefixes: Enter at least one prefix, one per line.

cidr.rfcNote: /31 and /32 reserve nothing: every address is usable (RFC 3021).
cidr.reservedNote: The first address is the network and the last is the broadcast; both are reserved, so usable = total − 2.

cipher.name: Cipher Suite Decoder
cipher.blurb: Enter a TLS cipher suite, as an IANA name, an OpenSSL or GnuTLS name, or a hex code point, to break it into its key exchange, authentication, cipher, mode, and MAC, with a plain-language security read-out and the official IANA recommendation. Runs entirely in your browser against a bundled copy of the IANA registry.
cipher.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally against a bundled copy of the IANA TLS Cipher Suite registry. Nothing is sent anywhere.
cipher.notInRegistryNote: This name parsed structurally but was not found in the bundled IANA registry snapshot, so its code point and cross-names are not shown.

errors.empty: Enter a cipher suite to decode.
errors.format: That is not a cipher suite. Enter an IANA name (TLS_...), an OpenSSL name, or a hex code point such as 0x1301.
errors.unknown: That is a valid code-point format, but it is not an assigned cipher suite in the IANA registry.

reasons.nullCipher: NULL cipher: the traffic is not encrypted at all.
reasons.anon: Anonymous key exchange: the peer is not authenticated, so it gives no protection against an active man-in-the-middle.
reasons.export40: Export-grade 40-bit key: trivially brute-forced, a relic of 1990s export rules.
reasons.rc4: RC4 stream cipher: a biased keystream makes it broken, and RFC 7465 prohibits it for TLS.
reasons.singleDes: Single DES with a 56-bit key: brute-forceable in hours.
reasons.rc2: RC2: an obsolete 40/64-bit cipher that is not safe for modern use.
reasons.tripleDes: 3DES has a 64-bit block, which exposes it to the Sweet32 birthday attack (CVE-2016-2183). RFC 8429 deprecates it.
reasons.md5Mac: MD5 MAC: MD5 is cryptographically broken.
reasons.cbcMac: CBC with an HMAC-SHA1 or MD5 MAC uses MAC-then-encrypt, which is exposed to padding-oracle attacks such as BEAST and Lucky13.
reasons.ccm8: CCM_8 truncates the authentication tag to 8 bytes, weakening integrity. IANA does not mark it Recommended.
reasons.noFs: No forward secrecy: with a static key exchange, one compromised long-term key can decrypt past sessions. Modern guidance requires ECDHE or DHE.
reasons.legacyCipher: {value} is a legacy cipher that is deprecated for TLS.
reasons.aead: AEAD cipher (authenticated encryption): confidentiality and integrity in one primitive, with no separate MAC to misuse.
reasons.fs: Forward secrecy: an ephemeral key exchange keeps past traffic safe even if the long-term key is later compromised.
reasons.tls13: TLS 1.3 suite: only modern AEAD ciphers, with the key exchange negotiated separately and always ephemeral.
reasons.ianaD: IANA marks this suite "D" (discouraged): it SHOULD NOT or MUST NOT be used, depending on the situation.
reasons.ianaN: IANA does not mark this suite "Recommended": it has limited applicability or has not been through the IETF consensus process.
reasons.signalling: Signalling suite (SCSV): not an actual cipher, it carries a protocol signal such as fallback protection or renegotiation info.

groups.title: Key-exchange groups
groups.intro: TLS negotiates the actual key-agreement group separately from the cipher suite, in the supported_groups extension. As "harvest now, decrypt later" drives the move to post-quantum key agreement, the hybrid groups below pair a classical curve with ML-KEM.
groups.foot: X25519MLKEM768 is the hybrid most browsers now send by default.

csr-decoder.name: CSR decoder
csr-decoder.blurb: Decode a PKCS#10 certificate signing request to read its subject, public key, requested SANs and extensions, and attributes — entirely in your browser.
csr-decoder.inputLabel: Certificate signing request (PEM, base64, or hex)
csr-decoder.runsLocally: Everything runs in your browser; the CSR is never uploaded.
csr-decoder.notCertNote: A CSR is a request, not a certificate. It has no serial number, no issuer, and no validity dates — only what the requester is asking a CA to certify.
csr-decoder.noSubject: No subject name (this CSR relies on requested SANs).
csr-decoder.err_empty: Paste a certificate signing request to decode.
csr-decoder.err_format: That does not look like a PEM, base64, or hex CSR.
csr-decoder.err_der: The DER structure could not be parsed.
csr-decoder.err_structure: This parses as DER but is not a PKCS#10 certification request.

curl-command-builder.name: curl command builder
curl-command-builder.blurb: Pick any of the 27 protocols curl speaks, fill in protocol-aware fields, and get the exact command with every flag explained and safety warnings surfaced. The inverse of the HTTP request translator: that one reads a command, this one writes it.

ui.passNote: Leaving the password empty makes curl prompt for it at run time, keeping it out of your shell history.
ui.insecure: Skip TLS verification (-k), with a warning
ui.err_host: Enter a host to build the command.
ui.err_path: Enter a local file path for the file:// URL.
ui.windowsNote: Quoting targets POSIX shells (bash, zsh). cmd.exe and PowerShell quote differently; run these as written in Git Bash or WSL.
ui.privacy: Runs entirely in your browser. Nothing you type is sent anywhere and no request is ever executed.

http.name: HTTP
http.desc: The web's transfer protocol. curl speaks HTTP/0.9 through HTTP/3 and gives you full control over method, headers, body, cookies, and authentication.

https.name: HTTPS
https.desc: HTTP inside TLS: the same request model with the transport encrypted and the server authenticated by certificate.

ws.name: WebSocket
ws.desc: A bidirectional, TCP-like channel set up over a plain HTTP request. The ws scheme is the cleartext flavor.

wss.name: WebSocket Secure
wss.desc: The same bidirectional WebSocket channel established over HTTPS, so the whole session runs inside TLS.

ftp.name: FTP
ftp.desc: The classic File Transfer Protocol: downloads, uploads, and directory listings. Starts in cleartext; the session can upgrade to TLS.

ftps.name: FTPS
ftps.desc: FTP with an implicit TLS layer from the first byte. Historically awkward through firewalls because of its separate data connections.

sftp.name: SFTP
sftp.desc: The SSH File Transfer Protocol carried over SSH version 2: one encrypted connection for both commands and data.

scp.name: SCP
scp.desc: The classic SSH copy protocol over SSH version 2. Plain file-copy semantics; SFTP is the richer sibling.

tftp.name: TFTP
tftp.desc: Trivial FTP over UDP: tiny, unauthenticated, cleartext by design. The firmware and network-boot workhorse.

smb.name: SMB
smb.desc: Windows file sharing. curl supports SMB version 1 for uploading and downloading files on a share.

smbs.name: SMBS
smbs.desc: SMB wrapped in TLS: the same version 1 file semantics with the transport encrypted.

file.name: Local file
file.desc: Local file access through a URL, no network at all. Handy for testing pipelines with real curl syntax.

smtp.name: SMTP
smtp.desc: Mail submission: uploading a message to an SMTP server means sending it. The envelope sender and recipients are set with dedicated flags.

smtps.name: SMTPS
smtps.desc: SMTP with implicit TLS from the first byte, conventionally on port 465.

pop3.name: POP3
pop3.desc: Retrieve mail from a POP3 server: downloading means fetching a message. Starts cleartext and can upgrade with STLS.

pop3s.name: POP3S
pop3s.desc: POP3 inside implicit TLS on port 995.

imap.name: IMAP
imap.desc: Read mail and mailboxes on an IMAP server; the URL selects the mailbox and message. Starts cleartext, can upgrade to TLS.

imaps.name: IMAPS
imaps.desc: IMAP inside implicit TLS on port 993.

mqtt.name: MQTT
mqtt.desc: The IoT publish-subscribe protocol, version 3. Downloading a topic subscribes to it; posting data publishes on it.

mqtts.name: MQTTS
mqtts.desc: MQTT carried inside TLS, conventionally on port 8883.

ldap.name: LDAP
ldap.desc: Directory lookups: the URL encodes the base DN, attributes, scope, and filter in one line.

ldaps.name: LDAPS
ldaps.desc: LDAP inside implicit TLS on port 636.

dict.name: DICT
dict.desc: The dictionary server protocol: a URL like /d:word looks the word up on the server.

gopher.name: Gopher
gopher.desc: The pre-web document protocol, still alive in niches. Retrieves menus and documents.

gophers.name: GopherS
gophers.desc: Gopher over TLS, a modern extension to the old protocol.

rtsp.name: RTSP
rtsp.desc: The Real Time Streaming Protocol: curl supports RTSP 1.0 downloads for talking to streaming media servers.

telnet.name: Telnet
telnet.desc: An interactive cleartext session: curl sends what it reads on stdin and prints what the server returns. Useful for poking raw TCP services.

opt.headOnly: -I asks for headers only (a HEAD request); no body is transferred.
opt.method: -X sets the request method explicitly; curl only needs it when the method cannot be inferred.
opt.httpVersion: Pins the HTTP version instead of letting curl negotiate one.
opt.header: -H adds one request header exactly as written.
opt.user: -u sends credentials; with no password given, curl prompts for it.
opt.data: -d sends this data as the request body: on HTTP it implies POST, on MQTT it publishes to the topic.
opt.dataUrlencode: --data-urlencode sends the data URL-encoded, safe for arbitrary characters.
opt.form: -F builds a multipart/form-data body; a leading @ uploads the file at that path.
opt.upload: -T uploads the given local file to the URL (PUT on HTTP, STOR on FTP, the message on SMTP).
opt.cookie: -b sends the given cookie string, or reads cookies from a file.
opt.cookieJar: -c writes the cookies received during the transfer to this file.
opt.proxy: -x sends the transfer through this proxy.
opt.resolve: --resolve pins host:port to a specific address, bypassing DNS for that name.
opt.cacert: --cacert verifies the server against this CA bundle instead of the system store.
opt.connectTimeout: --connect-timeout caps how long establishing the connection may take, in seconds.
opt.maxTime: --max-time caps the whole transfer duration, in seconds.
opt.retry: --retry retries a transient failure this many times, with backoff.
opt.followRedirects: -L follows HTTP redirects to the final location.
opt.compressed: --compressed asks for a compressed response and transparently decompresses it.
opt.listOnly: -l asks for a names-only listing (FTP directory or mailbox).
opt.outputRemote: -O saves the download under its remote file name.
opt.outputFile: -o saves the download to the given file name.
opt.include: -i includes the response headers in the output.
opt.verbose: -v narrates the whole exchange, including connection and TLS details.
opt.silent: -s silences the progress meter and error output.

warn.insecure: -k disables TLS certificate verification: the connection is encrypted but the peer is not authenticated. Fine against a lab box, dangerous anywhere else.
warn.cleartext: This protocol starts in cleartext: everything, including any credentials, crosses the network unencrypted unless the session upgrades to TLS.
warn.passOnCli: The password is part of the command line: it can land in shell history and show in the process list. Prefer -u user alone and let curl prompt, or use a .netrc file.
warn.dataDefaultCt: With -d and no Content-Type header, curl sends application/x-www-form-urlencoded. If this body is JSON, add a Content-Type: application/json header.
warn.considerSslReqd: This protocol can upgrade to TLS (STARTTLS style). Consider adding --ssl-reqd so the transfer fails rather than continue in cleartext.
warn.telnetInteractive: telnet: opens an interactive session; curl sends what it reads on stdin and prints what the server returns.
warn.smbV1: curl speaks SMB version 1, which many modern servers disable. Expect this to work mainly against legacy shares.

cvss-vector-decoder.name: CVSS vector decoder
cvss-vector-decoder.blurb: Paste a CVSS v3.1 or v3.0 vector and get the score computed and mapped to a severity, with every metric spelled out. Local and offline; nothing is sent anywhere.
cvss-vector-decoder.privacy: The vector is scored in your browser. Nothing is uploaded.
cvss-vector-decoder.notCvss: Paste a CVSS v3.0 or v3.1 vector string to decode it.
cvss-vector-decoder.parseError: That does not parse as a CVSS vector ({detail}).
cvss-vector-decoder.unsupportedVersion: This is a CVSS {version} vector. This decoder computes CVSS v3.0 and v3.1 only. CVSS v4.0 uses a different scoring model, and CVSS v2 is retired.

warnings.title: Notes
warnings.no-version-prefix: No CVSS version prefix was found. Scored as CVSS v3.1; add a CVSS:3.1/ or CVSS:3.0/ prefix to be explicit.
warnings.duplicate-metric: A metric appears more than once. The last value given was used.
warnings.incomplete-base: Some mandatory base metrics are missing, so no score can be computed. All eight of AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, and A are required.

diff.name: Text Diff
diff.blurb: Compare two blocks of text and see exactly what changed, line by line, with inline word-level highlighting on edited lines. Optionally ignore whitespace or case. Everything is computed in your browser; your text is never sent anywhere.
diff.emptyState: Paste text in both boxes to compare them.
diff.tooLarge: These inputs are too large to diff in the browser. Try comparing smaller sections.

dig-output-explainer.name: dig output explainer
dig-output-explainer.blurb: Paste real dig output and get a decoded, explained breakdown: the header and flags, the EDNS OPT pseudo-section, every record in each section, and the query stats. Parsed entirely in your browser, nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.

input.label: Paste dig output
input.placeholder:
; <<>> DiG 9.18.1 <<>> example.com A
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: ...

dig-output-explainer.privacy: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is resolved or sent anywhere.
dig-output-explainer.notDig: This does not look like dig output. Paste the full text dig prints, including the ;; ->>HEADER<<- line.

warnings.title: Worth noticing
warnings.nonzero-rcode: The status is not NOERROR, so this is not a normal successful answer. See the status meaning above.
warnings.truncated-tc: The TC (truncated) flag is set: the UDP answer did not fit and is incomplete. dig normally retries over TCP.
warnings.noerror-nodata: Status is NOERROR but there are no answer records (NODATA): the name exists, but not for this record type.
warnings.looks-like-query: The QR flag is not set, so this looks like a query rather than a response.
warnings.dnssec-requested-not-validated: DNSSEC was requested (the DO flag is set) but the AD flag is not, so the resolver did not return validated data.

opt.title: EDNS (OPT pseudo-section)
opt.help: EDNS(0) extends DNS over UDP: it advertises a larger UDP payload size and carries flags such as DO (DNSSEC OK) and options like COOKIE.

do-explainer-validator.name: DO declaration explainer + validator
do-explainer-validator.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/declarative-onboarding and it reads it back: whether it is a DO request wrapper or a bare Device declaration, the top-level options, and the Common tenant's class-objects grouped by the phase DO onboards them in, with every class named and explained. It also checks the structural rules F5 documents and flags the gotchas that bite in production, from the DO 1.36 allowService default change to a root user missing its oldPassword. DO is the sibling of AS3: DO does the L1-L3 onboarding, AS3 the L4-L7 services.
do-explainer-validator.inputLabel: A DO declaration (Device JSON) or a class DO request wrapper
do-explainer-validator.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
do-explainer-validator.empty: Paste a DO declaration to see it explained.
do-explainer-validator.scopeNote: This is a structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator. It never contacts a BIG-IP or BIG-IQ and nothing you paste leaves the browser. A declaration that passes here can still be rejected by DO itself.
do-explainer-validator.clusterNote: This declaration includes clustering classes, so it onboards one member of a high-availability pair or group.
do-explainer-validator.walkOrderNote: Grouped by the phase DO effectively onboards them in: licensing and provisioning gate the modules, system identity is foundational, networking builds the data plane, then clustering joins the box to its peers.
do-explainer-validator.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog, but still reported)

req.noDeclaration: no declaration found in the wrapper

finding.notDo: This is not a DO declaration. Paste a Device declaration, or a class DO request wrapper.
finding.deviceOnly: This is a bare Device declaration, as POSTed straight to a BIG-IP (a class DO wrapper with a targetHost is used against a BIG-IQ).
finding.missingSchema: No schemaVersion. A DO declaration should declare the schemaVersion it targets.
finding.noCommon: No Common tenant found. A DO declaration must contain a single tenant named Common.
finding.tenantNotCommon: The tenant is named "{name}", but DO requires the tenant to be named Common.
finding.emptyCommon: The Common tenant has no onboarding objects.
finding.hostnameConflict: hostname is set on both Common and a System class. They are mutually exclusive; set it in one place only.
finding.rootOldPassword: The root user "{name}" has no oldPassword. Changing the root password requires its existing oldPassword.
finding.allowServiceDefault: The self IP "{name}" sets no allowService. Since DO 1.36 the default changed from `default` to `none`, so it now allows no services unless you set them explicitly.
finding.asyncNote: async is true, so DO returns a 202 with a task id immediately; poll the task with GET until it completes.

epoch.name: Unix time converter
epoch.blurb: Type a Unix timestamp — seconds, milliseconds, microseconds, or nanoseconds, detected automatically — or an ISO-8601 date, and read it back in every common format. All in your browser.
epoch.runsLocally: Converted in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.
epoch.scopeNote: Pure date math. The unit is guessed from the number's size; correct it if needed.
epoch.err_empty: Enter a timestamp or date to begin.
epoch.err_invalid: That is not a Unix timestamp or an ISO-8601 date.
epoch.err_outOfRange: That timestamp is outside the representable date range.

f5-afm-rule-context.name: AFM rule-context & match explainer
f5-afm-rule-context.blurb: Walk a packet through AFM's documented context order and watch the semantics that decide real outcomes: accept passes one context and the traffic is processed again at the next, only accept-decisively ends the walk, ICMP rules at a virtual server or self IP are ignored, and staged policies log without enforcing. A lone policy gets the redundant-and-conflicting audit the system itself defines.
f5-afm-rule-context.inputLabel: Contexts + policies + a packet, a lone policy, or "contexts" / "actions"
f5-afm-rule-context.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
f5-afm-rule-context.postureNote: Explains and simulates documented processing; nothing here probes or touches any device.

f5-apm-session-variable-reference.name: APM session-variable reference
f5-apm-session-variable-reference.blurb: The Session Variables chapter as a pattern-aware lookup: paste session.ad.last.attr.memberOf and it resolves against the chapter's own templates, paste an mcget expression and every reference inside is explained with the secure audit riding along, the classic empty-value trap named exactly: a bare mcget on session.logon.last.password reads back empty, because secure variables require -secure.
f5-apm-session-variable-reference.inputLabel: A variable, an expression, a family, or "variables"
f5-apm-session-variable-reference.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.

f5-apm-sso-explainer.name: APM SSO method explainer
f5-apm-sso-explainer.blurb: The eight SSO methods APM's own chapter defines, each card carrying the verdict that decides outages: a misconfigured SSO object for any non-form method can disable SSO for every method in that user's session; Form Based and Forms - Client Initiated are the only exempt ones. Kerberos ships its full prerequisite list, including the line worth framing: APM Kerberos SSO does not need or use a keytab file.
f5-apm-sso-explainer.inputPlaceholder: kerberos · ntlmv2 · forms · fbci · basic · saml · oauth · methods
f5-apm-sso-explainer.runsLocally: Runs locally in your browser. Nothing you type leaves this page.

f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.name: AWAF declarative-policy explainer
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.blurb: Paste a BIG-IP Advanced WAF declarative security policy (JSON) and get a section-by-section, plain-language reading grounded in F5's published schema, with security callouts that read the values: transparent enforcement means monitor-only, plus signature staging, Data Guard off, and cookies missing Secure or HttpOnly. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.runsLocally: Runs entirely in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded or leaves the page.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.empty: Paste a declarative WAF policy above to see it explained.
f5-awaf-declarative-policy-explainer.notRecognized: This does not look like a declarative WAF policy

f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.name: AWAF evasion-technique explainer
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.blurb: Type a sub-violation name or "evasions" for F5's eight evasion sub-violations explained, each with its default and the encoding trick it catches, or paste the evasions block of a declarative WAF policy to read each one back as enabled or disabled with the Multiple-decoding pass count. Grounded in F5 K7929; runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.inputLabel: A sub-violation name, "evasions", or a policy's evasions block
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.inputPlaceholder:
Type "evasions" for all eight, or a name like "Multiple decoding", or paste:
{ "blocking-settings": { "evasions": [ { "description": "Multiple decoding", "enabled": true, "maxDecodingPasses": 3 } ] } }
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.empty: Type "evasions" for F5's eight sub-violations, a single name, or paste an evasions block to read it back.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.nameMiss: That is not one of F5's eight evasion sub-violations. Try "evasions" for the full list.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.passesDefault: Multiple-decoding passes: not set, so the default of {value} applies.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.bridgeNote: Several of these are the same decode operations this toolbox performs: %u, bare-byte and the %XX in Bad unescape are the Base64/Percent codec's territory, and Multiple decoding is repeated percent-decoding. The related tool on each card lets you see the decode by hand.

notes.notName: That is not JSON and not a known sub-violation name.
notes.parseError: That looks like JSON but could not be parsed
notes.noBlock: This JSON has no evasions array. Paste the evasions block, or a policy whose blocking-settings contains one.
notes.allDefault: No evasion sub-violation was explicitly set here, so every one takes its template default (all eight are Enabled by default).
notes.disabled: Disabled here: {names}. A disabled sub-violation means that normalization or check is skipped, so its evasion trick can slip past the signatures.
notes.passesRange: Multiple-decoding passes is {value}, outside the schema's 2-to-5 range. The system would reject or clamp this value.
notes.passesRaised: Multiple-decoding passes is raised to {value} (default is 3). More passes catch deeper nested encoding but cost slightly more processing.
notes.unknownEntry: An evasions entry has an unrecognized description ({description}); it is not one of F5's eight sub-violations.
notes.learnGated: Learning is disabled on the parent "Evasion technique detected" violation, so per the schema these sub-violations are not learned even when a request triggers them.

pct-u.name: %u decoding
pct-u.what: Performs Microsoft %u-style Unicode decoding on the URI and parameters, so a character written as %u00XX is resolved to what it really is before the signatures see it.
pct-u.catches: Attacks hidden behind Microsoft %u Unicode escapes, a classic way to write a metacharacter so a naive filter misses it.

apache-ws.name: Apache whitespace
apache-ws.what: Detects the whitespace bytes ASCII 9, 11, 12 and 13 in the URI, control characters some servers treat as separators.
apache-ws.catches: Requests that smuggle payloads past a filter using unusual whitespace that the origin server tolerates.

bad-unescape.name: Bad unescape
bad-unescape.what: Detects illegal hex encoding, a percent sign not followed by two valid hex digits (for example %RR).
bad-unescape.catches: Malformed percent-escapes crafted so the WAF and the server disagree on what the sequence decodes to.

bare-byte.name: Bare byte decoding
bare-byte.what: Detects higher-ASCII bytes, raw values above 127 sent unescaped in the request.
bare-byte.catches: Non-ASCII bytes injected directly to represent characters a signature is watching for, without the encoding that would reveal them.

dir-traversal.name: Directory traversals
dir-traversal.what: Detects path-traversal patterns such as ../ that walk up the directory tree.
dir-traversal.catches: Attempts to escape the intended path and reach files elsewhere on the server, the core of a path-traversal attack.

iis-backslash.name: IIS backslashes
iis-backslash.what: Normalizes the backslash \ to a forward slash /, the way IIS treats the two as equivalent in a path.
iis-backslash.catches: Payloads that use \ where the signatures expect /, relying on the server to fold them together after the WAF has looked.

iis-unicode.name: IIS Unicode codepoints
iis-unicode.what: Decodes IIS-specific %u code-point mappings from the Windows-1252 code page.
iis-unicode.catches: The IIS Unicode-escape evasions that famously let attackers write traversal and metacharacters in a form only IIS would decode.

multi-decode.name: Multiple decoding
multi-decode.what: Decodes the URI and parameter values repeatedly, up to the configured number of passes, so payloads hidden under several layers of encoding are unwrapped before inspection.
multi-decode.catches: Double- and triple-encoded payloads, where a value is percent-encoded more than once so a single-pass decoder never reveals the real characters.

f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.name: AWAF false-positive triage
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.blurb: The flip side of the poisoning estimator: relax a genuine Advanced WAF false positive correctly, with scope, and stop before relaxing a real attack. Pick a violation category, its average violation rating, and whether it is enforced, staged, or transparent, and get F5's rating-based verdict and the scoped fix. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented triage behaviour, not a probe. Read the violation, rating, and enforcement state from the request in Security > Event Logs > Application > Requests. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

cat.meta-char: Illegal meta character (in parameter, value, or URL)
cat.length: Illegal length (URL, query, POST data, request)
cat.xml-json-signature: Signature in XML or JSON payload

triage.likely-attack: Likely a real attack — do not relax

action.accept-if-confirmed: If you confirm it is legitimate, apply the scoped fix below (or accept the learning suggestion).
action.investigate-first: Examine the request in the event log first; a rating of 3 can go either way.
action.clear-do-not-relax: Clear the learning suggestion without changing the policy. Relaxing this would open a hole.

f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.remediationHeading: Scoped remediation (if it is a confirmed false positive)

rem.disableSignatureScoped: Disable the specific signature on the specific URL or parameter only, never for the whole policy.
rem.allowEntityBypass: Add the URL or parameter as an allowed entity so the check is bypassed just there.
rem.potentialFpDetection: Enable Potential False Positive Detection so the system learns request similarity and treats requests like the majority as benign, flagging outliers.
rem.stageSignature: Put the signature (back) into staging so it logs but does not block while you gather more traffic.
rem.allowMetaChar: Add the character to the allowed meta-characters of that parameter, value, or URL character set.
rem.addAllowedParameter: Add the parameter as an allowed parameter with the correct value type and length.
rem.addAllowedUrl: Add the URL to the allowed URLs.
rem.addAllowedFileType: Add the file type to the allowed file types.
rem.raiseLength: Raise the relevant length limit (URL, query string, POST data, or request) to fit legitimate traffic.
rem.markFileUploadParam: Add an explicit parameter with Value Type 'User-input value' and Data Type 'File Upload' so uploaded binary content is not scanned as text.
rem.attachContentProfile: Attach an XML or JSON content profile so the structured payload is parsed and signatures apply appropriately, rather than to raw bytes.
rem.allowCookieNotEnforced: Only if the application legitimately modifies the cookie, allow that specific cookie as not-enforced.
rem.investigateCookie: Cookie violations are usually real tampering; confirm the application actually changes the cookie before relaxing anything.
rem.adjustHttpComplianceSubcheck: Adjust the specific HTTP compliance sub-check only, if the deviation is legitimate for this application.
rem.urlOverride: Use a Microservices or URL-level override to relax the check for that one host and URL.

note.ratingBlocks: A rating of 4 or 5 blocks the request even when every detected violation has its Block flag off, because the combined rating marks it as a likely attack.
note.staged: This signature or entity is in staging: it logs but does not block. Treat the 'false positive' as a learning signal, validate over one to two weeks, then enforce.
note.transparent: The policy is in Transparent mode, so it blocks nothing. This is logged, not blocked; the fix matters for when you move to Blocking.
note.discipline: The rule that governs all of this: relax the policy only where a false positive actually occurred, never where a real attack caused the violation.
note.scopeIt: Always scope the fix to the specific URL or parameter. Disabling a signature or violation policy-wide trades one false positive for a blind spot everywhere else.
note.potentialFp: For attack-signature false positives, Potential False Positive Detection is the lightest-touch option: it lets the system decide by traffic similarity instead of you hand-disabling signatures.
note.unlearnable: Some rating-5 violations are unlearnable (for example Null in request): the policy will never relax them, so there is nothing to accept.

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.name: AWAF automatic-learning poisoning estimator
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.blurb: How many requests does an attacker need to drill a hole through your BIG-IP Advanced WAF policy when the Policy Builder is left in Automatic learning against untrusted traffic? Enter your Loosen thresholds and the attacker's resources; it computes the minimum sources, requests, and time to force one automatic relaxation, and gates on the rules that make it impossible. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented Policy Builder behaviour, not a probe. It never contacts a BIG-IP. Read your policy's real Loosen thresholds from Security > Application Security > Policy Building > Learning and Blocking Settings.

f.attackerTrusted: Attacker's IPs are in the Trusted IP list
f.ratingFactorHint: F5 requires more for higher ratings but does not publish the curve; set your own (1 = model only the threshold)

verdict.learning-disabled: Safe: learning is Disabled — the policy never changes itself.
verdict.manual: Safe: Manual mode — a human must accept every relaxation, so there is no automatic hole to drill.
verdict.unlearnable: Safe: a rating-5 violation is unlearnable — the Policy Builder never auto-relaxes it, no matter how much traffic arrives.
verdict.trusted-only-blocked: Safe: loosening is restricted to trusted traffic, so an untrusted attacker cannot reach the Loosen path.
verdict.insufficient-sources: Out of reach: the attacker does not control enough distinct sources to meet the Loosen threshold.
verdict.drillable: Drillable: this configuration can be relaxed automatically. Minimum attacker effort:

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.insufficientDetail: The Loosen rule needs {need} distinct sources; the attacker controls {have}. Raising this threshold is one of the mitigations below.

note.autoEnforce: In Automatic mode, a suggestion that reaches a 100% learning score is accepted and enforced with no human in the loop — including suggestions that disable a violation or widen an entity.
note.unlearnable: The most severe violations (for example Null in request, unparsable content, bad HTTP version) are unlearnable: no suggestion is ever created and the rating is fixed at 5, so the control cannot be laundered away this way.
note.ratingCost: At rating {rating}, F5 slows learning and requires more different sources and sessions before it relaxes. The higher the rating, the more expensive the poisoning.
note.trustedOneSession: Trusted clients relax the policy with as little as one session; untrusted traffic needs many more distinct sources over a period. That asymmetry is the anti-poisoning design.
note.emptyTrusted: If the Trusted IP list is empty, the system treats all traffic as untrusted — so an empty list does not make you safe, it makes the untrusted thresholds the only defence.
note.attackerTrusted: These IPs are trusted, so the fast trusted-traffic rules apply — this is why you must never place untrusted-reachable ranges in the Trusted IP list.
note.lowerBound: This request count is a documented lower bound (one distinct hit per required source/session). A real campaign needs more, because the pattern must recur to raise the score.

mit.manual: Set Learning Mode to Manual (or Disabled) so no suggestion is applied without a human accepting it. This is the clean default for production.
mit.trustedOnly: Loosen only from trusted traffic where the option exists (Track Site Changes), and build policies from a known-good Trusted IP range rather than live untrusted traffic.
mit.raiseThresholds: Raise the untrusted Loosen thresholds (different sources, sessions, time spread) so untrusted traffic effectively never relaxes the policy during the learning period.
mit.keepOut: Never put internet-reachable or attacker-reachable ranges in the Trusted IP list — trusted traffic relaxes the policy with a single session.
mit.disableAfter: Once the policy is built and stable, disable automatic policy building (or Track Site Changes) so the policy stops learning from live traffic.

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.untrustedActiveNote: This scenario loosens from untrusted traffic in Automatic mode — the exact configuration to avoid on an internet-facing policy.

f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.name: AWAF learning-suggestion interpreter
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.blurb: Ties the poisoning estimator and the false-positive triage together. Characterise a Traffic Learning suggestion and it tells you whether accepting it loosens or tightens the policy, whether a loosening is a false-positive fix or a security relaxation, and whether Automatic learning is about to enforce it for you. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.inputLabel: The learning suggestion you are weighing up
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented learning behaviour, not a probe. Read the action, learning score, and average violation rating from the Traffic Learning screen. It never contacts a BIG-IP and does not depend on the exact REST suggestion JSON.

action.add-entity: Add a valid entity (URL / parameter / file type / cookie)
action.add-meta-char: Allow a meta-character on an entity
action.relax-attribute: Relax an entity attribute (e.g. longer length)
action.disable-signature: Disable or relax an attack signature
action.remove-wildcard: Remove a wildcard, add explicit entities

assessment.add-legitimate: Adding a legitimate entity the policy had not learned
assessment.fp-fix: Loosening that resolves a genuine false positive
assessment.likely-relaxing-attack: This loosening would relax a real attack

note.scoreRatingInverse: The learning score rises as the violation rating falls, so the lowest-rated suggestions (the most likely false positives) reach auto-accept fastest. That is by design, and it is also exactly what an attacker feeding low-rated violations drives up.
note.autoEnforce: In Automatic learning, a suggestion that reaches a 100% learning score is accepted and enforced automatically, with no human step.
note.poisoningVector: This is the poisoning vector: a relaxing loosening from untrusted traffic climbing toward auto-accept in Automatic mode. Model how far it is with the poisoning estimator, and consider Manual learning or trusted-only loosening.
note.discipline: Relax the policy only where a false positive occurred, never where a real attack caused the violation. The violation rating is how you tell the two apart.
note.enforceCheckFp: Enforcing moves the entity from staging to blocking, so confirm it has generated no false positives during staging before you accept.
note.tighteningSafe: Tightening increases the policy's specificity, so accepting it generally improves security rather than weakening it.
note.manualHuman: In Manual learning a human must accept every suggestion, so nothing is enforced without your review.
note.scoreNearAccept: At a {score}% learning score this suggestion is close to being accepted; check it deliberately rather than letting it through.

f5-awaf-policy-diff.name: AWAF policy-diff hole checker
f5-awaf-policy-diff.blurb: Paste a before and an after declarative WAF policy and it classifies every security-relevant change as a relaxation or a tightening, then answers the question that matters after tuning: did this open a hole? It flags relaxations that widen protection beyond a single entity apart from a properly-scoped single-entity allow. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.inputLabel: Two declarative WAF policies to compare
f5-awaf-policy-diff.beforePlaceholder:
Paste the earlier policy JSON, e.g.
{ "policy": { "enforcementMode": "blocking", "data-guard": { "enabled": true } } }
f5-awaf-policy-diff.afterPlaceholder:
Paste the later policy JSON, e.g.
{ "policy": { "enforcementMode": "transparent", "data-guard": { "enabled": false } } }
f5-awaf-policy-diff.runsLocally: Compared in your browser. Neither policy is uploaded.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.scopeNote: A deterministic diff of the security-relevant sections F5's declarative policy schema defines. It reads enforcement mode, signature staging, X-Forwarded-For trust, Data Guard, CSRF, per-violation block flags, per-evasion enablement, and the URL / parameter / file-type entities. It never contacts a BIG-IP.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.empty: Paste a before and an after policy to see which changes opened a hole and which stayed scoped.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.parseError: One of the two inputs is not valid JSON. Check both policies and try again.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.notPolicy: One of the inputs does not look like a declarative WAF policy (expected a policy object, optionally wrapped in a "policy" key).

verdict.opened-hole: This opened a hole: a policy-wide relaxation is present
verdict.scoped-only: Scoped changes only: the widenings stay entity-scoped
verdict.no-change: No security-relevant change between the two policies

f5-awaf-policy-diff.policyWideHeading: Policy-wide relaxations (these widen beyond a single entity)
f5-awaf-policy-diff.scopedHeading: Scoped widenings (single-entity — the normal false-positive fix)

label.enforcementToTransparent: Enforcement switched to Transparent: the whole policy stops blocking, regardless of per-violation block flags.
label.enforcementToBlocking: Enforcement switched to Blocking: the policy now enforces its block flags.
label.sigStagingOn: Signature staging turned on: signatures log but no longer block until enforced.
label.sigStagingOff: Signature staging turned off: signatures now enforce.
label.trustXffOn: X-Forwarded-For trust turned on: the policy now trusts a client-supplied header for the source IP, which is spoofable.
label.trustXffOff: X-Forwarded-For trust turned off: the policy no longer trusts the client-supplied header.
label.dataGuardOff: Data Guard turned off: responses are no longer masked for sensitive data leakage.
label.dataGuardOn: Data Guard turned on: responses are masked for sensitive data.
label.wildcardAdded: Wildcard {type} added: {name} — this matches many, not one entity.

f5-awaf-request-log-triage.name: AWAF request-log triage
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.blurb: Paste an ASM request-log entry (syslog key-value or CEF) and it extracts the policy, the support ID for log correlation, the request status, the violation rating, the client IP, method, and URI, classifies each violation, and gives F5's rating-based verdict, then bridges to the false-positive triage tool. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.inputLabel: An ASM request-log entry (syslog key-value or CEF)
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.inputPlaceholder:
Paste a line like:
ASM:policy_name="prod",violations="Attack signature detected,Illegal meta character in parameter value",violation_rating="2",support_id="5268275531735896872",request_status="blocked",ip_client="192.0.2.9",method="GET",uri="/search"
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.empty: Paste an ASM request-log line (key-value or CEF) to parse it and triage its violations.

verdict.likely-fp: Rating says: likely a false positive
verdict.likely-attack: Rating says: likely a real attack
verdict.no-rating: No violation rating in this log

note.noRating: This log has no violation_rating field (older versions omit it). Open the request in Security > Event Logs > Application > Requests to read its rating, then triage from there.
note.ratingAttack: The violation rating is {rating}. A 4 or 5 is most likely a real attack and blocks even with Block flags off, so clear any learning suggestion rather than relaxing the policy.
note.ratingFp: The violation rating is {rating}. A 1 or 2 is usually a genuine false positive, so if you confirm the request is legitimate you can apply the scoped fix in the false-positive triage tool.
note.ratingInvestigate: The violation rating is {rating}. A 3 is ambiguous; examine the request in the event log before you decide.
note.unparsed: This does not look like an ASM request-log line. Paste a syslog key-value line (starting with ASM:) or a CEF line (containing |F5|ASM|).
note.supportIdOpaque: The support ID is a correlation reference for finding this exact request in the event log. It does not encode the violations, so it cannot be decoded into them; this tool reads them from the log line instead.
note.bridge: Take each violation above into the false-positive triage tool with its category and the rating to get the scoped remediation.

f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.name: AWAF signature accuracy/risk interpreter
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.blurb: Read an attack signature's published Accuracy and Risk and it tells you how false-positive-prone it is, how damaging a real match would be, and the tuning move. F5 defines accuracy as false-positive susceptibility, so low accuracy means many false positives. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented signature properties, not a per-ID database. Read Accuracy, Risk, and Systems from the signature's own entry under Security > Options > Application Security > Attack Signatures. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

system.in-stack: Yes — a system my app runs
system.not-in-stack: No — a system my app does not run

quadrant.reliable-high-stakes: Reliable and high-stakes — do not relax

rec.fp-prone-low-stakes: This is the prime relax candidate. Low accuracy means frequent false positives and low risk means little is lost if you relax it, so disabling it on the specific URL or parameter (or leaving it in staging) is reasonable once you have confirmed a false positive.
rec.fp-prone-dangerous: Investigate the actual request before you relax anything. Low accuracy makes false positives likely, but the potential damage means a genuine match must not be waved through. Do not blindly disable this one.
rec.reliable-high-stakes: Do not relax this. High accuracy means a match is very likely a genuine attack, and the potential damage is severe. Treat matches as real and keep it enforcing.
rec.reliable-low-stakes: Reliable but low-stakes. Matches are trustworthy, so relax only with a confirmed false positive, and even then scope it to the specific URL or parameter.

accuracyFp.low: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a Low accuracy signature has a high likelihood of false positives.
accuracyFp.medium: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a Medium accuracy signature has some likelihood of false positives.
accuracyFp.high: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a High accuracy signature has a low likelihood of false positives.

note.systemNotInStack: This signature targets a system your application does not run, so it is pure noise here. Rather than disable it one policy at a time, scope your signature set to your actual systems (for example only the databases, web servers, and languages you use).
note.systemUnknown: Confirm whether this signature's system applies to your stack; a signature for a system you do not run only generates false positives.
note.staged: This signature is in staging: it logs but does not block. Treat any false positive as a learning signal, and enforce it only once you are confident it is clean.
note.transparent: The policy is in Transparent mode, so it blocks nothing. This is logged, not blocked; the accuracy and risk matter for when you move to Blocking.
note.highAccuracyLever: If low-accuracy false positives are frequent, let accuracy guide which signatures you enforce. Accuracy is a documented signature property and a signature-set filter criterion, so a signature set weighted toward higher-accuracy signatures produces fewer false positives, because F5 defines accuracy as false-positive susceptibility.
note.disciplineHighRisk: Because this signature guards against a damaging attack, do not disable it just because it is false-positive-prone. Check the actual request first; the cost of relaxing a real match here is high.
note.scopeIt: Scope any change to the specific URL or parameter, or filter the signature set. Disabling a signature policy-wide trades one false positive for a blind spot everywhere else.

f5-bigip-license-explainer.name: F5 BIG-IP license explainer
f5-bigip-license-explainer.blurb: Paste your /config/bigip.license, full file or a fragment, and get a plain-language reading: management flavor, licensing dates with the K7727 upgrade verdict, Registration Key and platform, active and optional modules, constraints, and feature tokens. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.inputPlaceholder: Paste the contents of /config/bigip.license here, the full file or any fragment.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed entirely in your browser. Nothing is uploaded, and key or signature values are never displayed.
f5-bigip-license-explainer.verifyNote: Field meanings follow the cited F5 articles; confirm licensing decisions against F5 documentation or support.

errors.notRecognized: That text does not look like a BIG-IP license. Expected markers such as active module, Registration Key, or the license file header.

dates.heading: Licensing dates
dates.k7727Blocked: Branches from {label} need a service check date of {date} or later, so a license reactivation would come first.

platform.heading: Platform and keys
platform.regKeyOdd: This registration key does not match the published 5-5-5-5-7 shape.
platform.decodedUnknown: Platform ID not in the vendored map; check F5 K9476, the hardware and software compatibility matrix.

modules.heading: Active modules
modules.optionalNote: Listed as licensable but dormant until licensed; provisioning may be possible while the feature stays inactive.

constraints.heading: Version and platform constraints
constraints.exvNote: Per F5 K42091606, the permitted BIG-IP range is read from the BIG-IP-style entries; F5's own example also lists low entries (5.*.* to 8.*.*) alongside them, below the K7727 table's 9.2.0 floor, without labeling what they refer to.
constraints.ceiling: Highest BIG-IP entry: {glob}. Version ceilings map to Version Plus SKU generations, per F5 K15643.

tokens.heading: Feature and limit tokens
tokens.count: {n} tokens found; values shown exactly as written.

validation.heading: Validation material
validation.privacy: Presence and length only; the values themselves are never displayed.

f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.name: BIG-IP persistence-cookie decoder
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.blurb: Decode an F5 BIGipServer persistence cookie into the backend pool member's IP and port, or encode one from an address and port. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.inputPlaceholder: Paste a cookie value, a name=value pair, or a full Set-Cookie line. Example: 1677787402.20480.0000
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.runsLocally: Decoded in your browser. The cookie is never sent anywhere.

errors.empty: Enter a BIGipServer cookie value to decode.
errors.unrecognized: This does not match any known BIG-IP persistence cookie format. Check that you pasted the cookie value, not just the name.

encodeErrors.address: Enter a valid IPv4 or IPv6 address.
encodeErrors.routedomain: Route domain must be a number between 0 and 65535.

reasons.INFO_DISCLOSURE: This cookie exposes a backend pool member's IP address and port to anyone who can read the response. Enable cookie encryption on the persistence profile to prevent this.
reasons.INTERNAL_ADDRESS: The decoded address is {value}, confirming an internal pool member and revealing part of your internal topology.
reasons.ROUTE_DOMAIN: The pool member sits in route domain {value}.
reasons.ENCRYPTED: This cookie is AES-encrypted. The pool member cannot be recovered without the BIG-IP's key, which is the recommended secure configuration.

f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.disclosureNote: Decoding uses only the public encoding scheme (F5 K6917). It confirms what any client can already read from the cookie; the fix is to encrypt the persistence cookie.
f5-bigip-persistence-cookie.encryptedNote: An encrypted persistence cookie is the recommended configuration. There is nothing to decode without the key, and that is by design.

encode.resultNote: This is the value a BIG-IP would set for that pool member using the default (unencrypted) encoding.

f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.name: BIG-IP tcpdump builder
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.blurb: Assemble a BIG-IP-correct tcpdump command from structured choices: the TMM interface syntax, flow detail, snaplen, file output, and a BPF filter. It formats the command for you to run; it captures nothing.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.runsLocally: The command is assembled in your browser. Nothing is captured, run, or sent anywhere.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.ifaceHint: 0.0 captures on every TMM. You can also name a VLAN, a tunnel, or a self-IP.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.detailHint: Adds the F5 noise/flow trailer to each packet. Higher detail prints flow and peer-flow information.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.bothSidesHint: Capture the client-side and server-side flows of the same connection.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.nameResHint: -n and -nn keep the capture fast and avoid reverse lookups that can stall it.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.countHint: Optional. Leave blank to capture until you stop it with Ctrl-C.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.fileNameHint: Required when sending a capture to F5 Support. Save under /var/tmp or /shared/tmp.
f5-bigip-tcpdump-builder.filterHint: Standard tcpdump/BPF syntax: host, net, port, src/dst, tcp/udp, and / or / not.

warnings.unfiltered-all-tmm: Capturing on 0.0 is not rate-limited. Add a filter (host or port) or a packet count (-c) so the capture file stays bounded.
warnings.snaplen-zero-modern: On BIG-IP 15.x and later, -s0 maps to the default snap length of 262144 bytes (kept for compatibility), so it still captures whole packets.
warnings.hex-with-writefile: -X only affects on-screen output. It has no effect when you also write a binary file with -w.
warnings.colon-in-iface-name: An interface name must not contain a colon on BIG-IP. The colon is reserved for the TMM detail suffix.

f5-service-check-date.name: F5 service check date
f5-service-check-date.blurb: Enter a BIG-IP version to get the minimum service check date its license must carry, or enter a service check date to see the newest version you can upgrade to. Based on F5's published License Check Date table (K7727); runs entirely in your browser.

f5-service-check-date.verifyNote: For production upgrade planning, always confirm against F5 K7727 or the target system's /etc/version_date file.

errors.empty: Enter a BIG-IP version or a service check date.
errors.format: That is not a BIG-IP version or a service check date. Enter a version like 17.1.3 or a date like 20230611.
errors.unknownVersion: That version is not in F5's License Check Date table.
errors.licenseNoDate: Found a Service check date label in the pasted text, but no date next to it. Check the paste or enter the date directly.

version.heading: Minimum service check date
version.forVersion: To upgrade to {label}, the license's service check date must be:
version.majorMinorNote: The check applies to the major.minor branch, so every maintenance and point release within {label} shares this date.
version.consequence: If the service check date is earlier than this, the system still boots after the upgrade but does not load its configuration until you reactivate the license.

date.heading: Upgrade eligibility
date.newestReachable: Newest version you can upgrade to
date.withinNote: Any maintenance or point release within {label} is included.
date.none: No version in the table is reachable with this service check date. Reactivate the license to obtain a newer service check date.
date.allReachable: This service check date reaches the newest version in the table; nothing is blocked.
date.blockedHeading: Newer branches you cannot reach yet
date.blockedHint: Each needs the license's service check date to be on or after the date shown. Reactivate the license first.
date.needsDate: needs a service check date of {date} or later

f5-service-check-date.pasteHint: You can also paste your /config/bigip.license contents or the tmsh show sys license output; the service check date is picked out automatically.

f5-ssl-profile-explainer.blurb: Paste a tmsh client-ssl or server-ssl profile and get its role, the TLS protocol matrix, and a security read covering chain, renegotiation, SNI, OCSP, and mutual TLS — all in your browser.

f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_empty: Paste an SSL profile to begin.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_noHeader: Expected a line like: ltm profile client-ssl /Common/name ...
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_noBody: No opening brace was found in the profile.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_tooLong: That input is too large to parse.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.err_invalid: That does not look like a client-ssl or server-ssl profile.
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.clientNote: A client-ssl profile terminates the client's TLS at the BIG-IP. The connection on to the pool member is re-encrypted only if a server-ssl profile is applied; with none, that leg is cleartext (SSL offload).
f5-ssl-profile-explainer.serverNote: A server-ssl profile lets the BIG-IP open a fresh TLS connection to the pool member (re-encryption). The client side is handled by a separate client-ssl profile.

f5xc-service-policy-explainer.name: F5XC service-policy explainer
f5xc-service-policy-explainer.blurb: Paste an F5 Distributed Cloud service_policy and see exactly how it matches: the server scope, the rule order, and every rule's action and conditions. iRules for XC, decoded.

input.label: F5 XC service_policy JSON
input.placeholder: Paste a service_policy spec (Create, Replace, or Get form, or a bare spec)...

f5xc-service-policy-explainer.privacy: Decoded locally in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere, and the policy is never evaluated against live traffic.
f5xc-service-policy-explainer.notPolicy: This does not look like an F5 XC service_policy. Expected a spec with a rule choice (rule_list, allow_all_requests, allow_list, deny_list, ...) or a server choice.

warnings.title: Worth a look
warnings.allow-all-policy: This policy allows all requests. Every request that reaches it is permitted, with no further checks.
warnings.allow-all-rule: A rule allows every request (ALLOW with no conditions). It matches everything that reaches it, so any rule below it is never evaluated. This is the common catch-all pattern, but confirm it is intentional.
warnings.no-rules: The rule list is empty. With no rules to match, requests fall through and the default is to deny.
warnings.expired-rule: A rule has an expiration timestamp in the past. It still exists in the config but is no longer applied.
warnings.case-sensitive-exact: An exact-value matcher has no LOWER_CASE or UPPER_CASE transformer, so the comparison is case-sensitive. '/Admin' will not match '/admin'.
warnings.inverted-matcher: A matcher is inverted (invert_matcher). It matches when the condition is NOT met, which is easy to misread.

algorithm.title: How the rules are evaluated
algorithm.FIRST_MATCH: First match: rules are evaluated top to bottom until one matches. That rule's action is applied and evaluation stops.
algorithm.DENY_OVERRIDES: Deny overrides: all DENY rules are evaluated before any ALLOW rule, so a matching DENY wins over a matching ALLOW.
algorithm.ALLOW_OVERRIDES: Allow overrides: all ALLOW rules are evaluated before any DENY rule, so a matching ALLOW wins over a matching DENY.
algorithm.defaultDeny: If no rule matches, the request does not match this policy and the next policy in the set is evaluated. If nothing allows it, the request is denied.

rule.matchesEverything: No conditions: this rule matches every request that reaches it.

logic.andAll: All of the following must be true (AND):

sourceList.title: Source list
sourceList.allow_list: A request is allowed if its source matches any of the following:
sourceList.deny_list: A request is denied if its source matches any of the following:

legacy.title: Referenced rules (legacy)
legacy.note: This policy points to separate service_policy_rule objects rather than defining rules inline. Their contents are not part of this spec.

hash.name: Hash Generator (SHA-1/256/384/512)
hash.blurb: Compute SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512 digests of any text, shown as hex and Base64, using the browser's native Web Crypto. Runs entirely in your browser.
hash.inputPlaceholder: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
hash.runsLocally: Hashing runs locally via Web Crypto. Nothing you type leaves your browser.
hash.sha1Note: SHA-1 is broken for collision resistance and unsuitable for security (signatures, certificates). It is fine only for non-security checksums and legacy interop.

hash-preimage-finder.name: Hash Preimage Finder
hash-preimage-finder.blurb: Watch a bounded, local brute-force search recover a weak hash input in seconds, or run out of keyspace on anything with real entropy. No wordlist, no table, just your browser. A demonstration of why fast, unsalted hashes fail.
hash-preimage-finder.hashPlaceholder: Paste an MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256 hash
hash-preimage-finder.detectHint: Paste a hex hash; the algorithm is detected from its length.
hash-preimage-finder.notDetected: Not a recognized hash. Expected 32 (MD5), 40 (SHA-1), or 64 (SHA-256) hex characters.
hash-preimage-finder.brokenNote: cryptographically broken; never use for new work
hash-preimage-finder.keyspaceBeyond: (search stops at {cap}; larger keyspaces are the point, they are infeasible)
hash-preimage-finder.foundIn: Recovered in {attempts} attempts, {ms} ms. A secret this weak offers no protection once the hash leaks.
hash-preimage-finder.exhaustedSub: The entire keyspace was searched with no match. Widen the alphabet or length, or, more likely, the input has more entropy than a short brute force can reach.
hash-preimage-finder.budgetSub: The search hit its budget with far more keyspace remaining (this alphabet and length span {n} candidates). This is exactly why length and randomness defeat brute force: the work grows faster than any attacker can keep up with.

teach.head: This is why weak hashes fall, and how storage is actually protected
teach.intro: A fast hash of a low-entropy input is trivially reversible by enumeration, as you just saw. Real password storage defends against this in three layers.

salt.title: Salting defeats precomputed tables
salt.body: A unique random salt per password makes every stored hash different, even for identical passwords. That is what breaks lookup services like CrackStation: a precomputed table would need rebuilding for every salt, so it becomes useless.

kdf.title: Slow KDFs defeat brute force
kdf.body: bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, and PBKDF2 are deliberately slow and tunable. Making each guess cost milliseconds instead of nanoseconds cuts an attacker's rate by six orders of magnitude or more, turning a seconds-long crack into millennia.

algo.title: Algorithm and length matter
algo.body: MD5 and SHA-1 are collision-broken and, like SHA-256, far too fast for storing secrets. Use a purpose-built password hash, and give secrets enough length and randomness that no keyspace search can reach them.

hash-preimage-finder.disclaimer: Everything runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded. This tool exists to demonstrate hash weakness and is bounded so it can only recover trivially weak inputs.

hmac.name: HMAC Generator (SHA-256/384/512)
hmac.blurb: Compute a keyed HMAC over a message with your secret key, shown as hex and Base64, via the browser's native Web Crypto. The same construction the JWT verifier uses for HS256. Your key never leaves your browser.
hmac.runsLocally: HMAC runs locally via Web Crypto. Your message and key never leave your browser.

struct.heading: How HMAC is built
struct.note: HMAC hashes the message twice. The key is XORed with a fixed inner pad before the first hash and with an outer pad before the second, and this two-pad construction is what prevents length-extension attacks. The key is first reduced to the hash's block size.

http-request-translator.name: HTTP request translator
http-request-translator.blurb: Paste a curl command and get it explained flag by flag, then translated to fetch, a raw HTTP request, HTTPie, and Python requests. Local and offline; the command is decoded in your browser and nothing is sent or run.
http-request-translator.privacy: Parsed locally in your browser. A curl command can carry tokens, cookies, or passwords, so nothing you paste is sent anywhere or executed.

err.empty: Paste a curl command to begin.
err.notCurl: This does not look like a curl command. Paste a command that starts with curl.
err.noUrl: No URL found. curl needs a target URL.

opt.head: Send a HEAD request (headers only, no body).
opt.get: Force GET and move data into the query string.
opt.include: Include the response headers in the output.
opt.verbose: Verbose output (show the request and response).
opt.proxy: Send the request through a proxy:
opt.output: Write the response body to a file:
opt.max-time: Maximum time for the whole operation, in seconds:
opt.connect-timeout: Maximum time allowed for the connection, in seconds:
opt.range: Request only part of the resource (byte range):
opt.retry: Retry the request on transient failures, up to:
opt.remote-name: Save the response using the remote file name.
opt.fail: Fail silently on server errors (no error-page body).

warnings.insecureTls: -k / --insecure disables TLS certificate verification. The connection is encrypted but not authenticated, so it is exposed to man-in-the-middle interception. Use it only against hosts you control.
warnings.plaintextHttp: This request uses http:// (not https://). Headers, body, and any credentials are sent in clear text.
warnings.credsInUrl: Credentials are embedded in the URL (user:pass@host). These can leak into logs and history; prefer a header or -u.
warnings.jsonButFormCt: The body looks like JSON, but -d does not set a JSON Content-Type. curl sends application/x-www-form-urlencoded by default, so the server may not parse it as JSON. Add -H "Content-Type: application/json".

ipv6.name: IPv6 Toolkit
ipv6.blurb: Parse an IPv6 address or prefix to see its canonical (RFC 5952) and fully expanded forms, special-use classification, prefix math, an EUI-64 MAC if present, and its ip6.arpa reverse-DNS name. Runs entirely in your browser.
ipv6.runsLocally: Parsing runs locally. The address never leaves your browser.

errors.empty: Enter an IPv6 address or prefix to decode.
errors.format: That is not a valid IPv6 address. Use eight hex groups, optionally compressed with :: once (for example 2001:db8::1).
errors.prefix: The prefix length is not valid. For IPv6 it must be between 0 and 128.

ipv6.prefixNote: Bits left of the boundary are fixed by the prefix; bits to the right identify the host within it.
ipv6.conventionNote: No prefix was given, so the dashed line marks the conventional /64 split: routing prefix and subnet on the left, the 64-bit interface identifier on the right.

iquery-protocol-explainer.blurb: Decode F5 BIG-IP DNS iqdump output and /var/log/gtm iQuery messages, or explain the iQuery architecture (mesh, port 4353, SSL trust, iqdump, gtmd, big3d). Runs locally.

iquery-protocol-explainer.inputPlaceholder: Paste iqdump output (the Connected to big3d header and the xml_connection stanza), or a /var/log/gtm line, or a topic: mesh, port, trust, iqdump, metrics, gtmd, big3d, vlan

json-formatter.runsLocally: Validated and formatted in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

json-yaml-convert.name: JSON ↔ YAML Converter
json-yaml-convert.blurb: Convert JSON to block-style YAML or YAML back to JSON, entirely in your browser. Parse errors point to the exact line and column, and conversion notes flag the lossy edges like dropped comments and expanded anchors.
json-yaml-convert.inputPlaceholder: Paste JSON or YAML to convert.
json-yaml-convert.runsLocally: Converted in your browser. Nothing is sent anywhere.

notes.COMMENTS_DROPPED: Comments were dropped. JSON has no way to represent them.
notes.ANCHORS_EXPANDED: Anchors and aliases were expanded inline; the repeated values are written out in full.
notes.MERGE_KEYS_LITERAL: YAML merge keys were kept as literal keys rather than flattened into the surrounding map.
notes.LARGE_NUMBER: This document has a very large number. Conversion uses JavaScript number precision, so values beyond about sixteen digits may be rounded; use the JSON formatter when exact digits matter.

jwks-explainer.name: JWKS explainer + key matcher
jwks-explainer.blurb: Paste a JSON Web Key Set to break down every key, flag any private material, and match a JWT to its key by kid. Nothing leaves your browser.
jwks-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. No jwks_uri is fetched.
jwks-explainer.jwtInputPlaceholder: Paste a JWT, or just its header
jwks-explainer.privateBanner: This set contains private key material. A JWKS should only ever contain public keys.
jwks-explainer.algMismatch: The JWT alg does not match the matched key alg.

diag.heading: How a key is matched by kid
diag.note: When a JWT arrives, the verifier reads its kid and finds the one key in the set with the same kid, then uses that key to check the signature. Each key needs a distinct kid for this to be unambiguous.

jwt.name: JWT Decoder & Verifier
jwt.blurb: Decode a JSON Web Token's header and claims, read its expiry and timing in plain language, and verify an HS256/384/512 signature with a pasted secret. Runs entirely in your browser.
jwt.runsLocally: Decoding and signature checks run locally. The token and secret never leave your browser.

errors.empty: Paste a JSON Web Token to decode.
errors.format: That does not look like a JWT. A token has three base64url parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature.
errors.header: The header is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.
errors.payload: The payload is not valid. It should be base64url-encoded JSON.

verify.label: Verify HMAC signature (HS256/384/512)
verify.invalid: Signature does not match this secret.
verify.algNone: This token is unsecured (alg: none), so there is no signature to verify.
verify.unsupportedAlg: In-browser verification covers HS256/384/512 only. {alg} is asymmetric and needs a public key.
verify.hint: The secret is used only to compute an HMAC in your browser. It is never sent anywhere.

struct.heading: Anatomy of a JWT
struct.note: The header and payload are only base64url-encoded, not encrypted, so anyone can read them. The signature is computed over the header and payload joined by a dot, and is what a verifier checks.

tools.lede: Small, fast, privacy-first utilities that run entirely in your browser. Nothing you type is sent anywhere.

nslookup-output-explainer.name: nslookup output explainer
